title: Deep Water by William Douglas
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Conquering fear through determination, willpower, and systematic effort. The lesson contrasts fear of death with fear itself.

summary:
This is an autobiographical account of how William Douglas conquered his lifelong fear of water. His phobia started at the age of three or four when a wave knocked him down at a California beach and buried him underwater. Though his father laughed, the terror stayed. Years later, at ten or eleven, he decided to learn swimming at the YMCA pool in Yakima. It was considered safe, with only two or three feet of water at the shallow end. One day when he was alone, an eighteen-year-old boy picked him up and tossed him into the deep end as a prank.

Douglas sank to the bottom. Though terrified, he planned to hit the bottom, push up, and float like a cork. But when he came up, he swallowed water and sank again. He tried twice more. The third time, he stopped trying. His legs felt paralyzed, his lungs ached, and blackness swept over him. When he woke up, he was lying beside the pool, vomiting. The terror never left him after that. He avoided water for years, and it ruined his fishing, boating, and swimming.

Finally, he decided to get an instructor. The instructor put a belt around him, attached a rope, and taught him piece by piece: how to exhale underwater, raise his nose, and kick his legs. It took months to build confidence. But small traces of fear remained, so Douglas tested himself in Lake Wentworth, Triggs Island, and Warm Lake. When he felt the terror leave him, he knew he had conquered it.

He concludes that there is peace in death, but terror only in the fear of death. The experience taught him the meaning of Roosevelt’s words: “All we have to fear is fear itself.”

qa.q: What is the “misadventure” that William Douglas speaks about?
qa.a: The "misadventure" occurred at the Y.M.C.A. swimming pool when Douglas was ten or eleven years old. While sitting alone on the edge of the pool waiting for others, a big, well-built eighteen-year-old boy picked him up and playfully tossed him into the nine-foot-deep end. Douglas landed in a sitting position, swallowed water, and went straight to the bottom, nearly drowning.

qa.q: What were the series of emotions and fears that Douglas experienced when he was thrown into the pool? What plans did he make to come to the surface?
qa.a: On his way down, Douglas was deeply frightened but kept his presence of mind. He planned that when his feet hit the bottom, he would make a big upward jump, bob to the surface like a cork, lie flat on it, and paddle to the edge. However, when his plan failed, he grew panicky, felt suffocated, and was seized by a stark, paralyzing terror that froze his screams and stiffened his limbs until he finally blacked out.

qa.q: How did this experience affect him?
qa.a: The near-death experience left Douglas physically weak, trembling, and deeply traumatized. For days, a haunting fear stayed in his heart, making him feel wobbly in the knees and sick to his stomach at the slightest exertion. He developed a severe aversion to water, avoided it for years, and found his fishing, canoeing, boating, and swimming trips completely ruined by the recurring panic.

qa.q: Why was Douglas determined to get over his fear of water?
qa.a: Douglas was determined to conquer his fear because it severely restricted his life and deprived him of fundamental joys. The handicap followed him everywhere, completely ruining his fishing trips for landlocked salmon, bass, and trout, and making it impossible for him to enjoy canoeing, boating, and swimming with others.

qa.q: How did the instructor “build a swimmer” out of Douglas?
qa.a: The instructor trained Douglas methodically over several months. He attached a rope and belt to Douglas via a pulley to practice moving across the pool safely. He then taught him how to exhale under water and inhale by raising his nose, followed by weeks of practicing leg kicks at the side of the pool. Piece by piece, the instructor perfected each skill before integrating them into a complete swimmer.

qa.q: How did Douglas make sure that he conquered the old terror?
qa.a: To ensure his fear was truly gone, Douglas tested himself alone in different waters. He first swam lengths completely alone in the pool, deliberately confronting the tiny remnants of terror that arose. He then dived off Triggs Island and swam two miles across Lake Wentworth, and later dived into Warm Lake at Conrad Meadows, swimming to the other shore and back to completely validate his victory.

qa.q: How does Douglas make clear to the reader the sense of panic that gripped him as he almost drowned? Describe the details that have made the description vivid.
qa.a: Douglas uses vivid sensory details and raw psychological descriptions to recreate his intense panic. He describes his lungs as "ready to burst," his head throbbing, and his hands clutching desperately at nothing but "dirty yellow water". The depiction of his legs hanging as "dead weights, paralysed and rigid," alongside his screams being "frozen" in his throat, makes the reader directly experience the suffocating, stark terror of his near-drowning.

qa.q: How did Douglas overcome his fear of water?
qa.a: Douglas overcame his fear through systematic, disciplined training under a professional instructor, followed by personal willpower. After practicing breathing, kicking, and strokes for months with a safety rope, he took it upon himself to push his boundaries. By swimming long distances in natural lakes like Lake Wentworth and Warm Lake entirely alone, he actively confronted and mocked his residual fears until they vanished completely.

qa.q: Why does Douglas as an adult recount a childhood experience of terror and his conquering of it? What larger meaning does he draw from this experience?
qa.a: Douglas recounts this deeply personal event to convey a universal psychological truth about human courage. The larger meaning he draws is that "In death there is peace. There is terror only in the fear of death," echoing President Roosevelt's words, "All we have to fear is fear itself". Having experienced both the physical sensation of dying and the mental terror it creates, his ultimate triumph left him with an intensified will to live and a profound sense of absolute freedom.
